explanation: The requirement states that both prop_0 and prop_1 must hold. In LTL, if something must always be true (globally), we use the 'G' operator. So, we want 'prop_0 and prop_1' to always be true.
The requirement says that both prop_0 and prop_1 should hold. By default, such top-level requirements are interpreted as invariants; that is, globally, prop_0 and prop_1 must always be true.

The requirement states that the system shall measure (implied by the context) and prop_1. Since only 'prop_1' is an atomic proposition, and 'measure' is not defined, we'll treat 'prop_1' as the property to be satisfied. The requirement can be interpreted as 'eventually, prop_1 will be true'. Thus, in LTL, we write: F prop_1.
The requirement states that the system must satisfy both prop_0 and prop_1, possibly meaning all the time (globally) these should hold. In LTL, this is usually interpreted as 'G (prop_0 & prop_1)'.
The requirement states that both prop_0 and prop_1 should always hold. In LTL, this is expressed as 'Globally (prop_0 AND prop_1)'.

The requirement is that the system must (i.e., always) measure (expressed as 'measure') and prop_1 must hold. Assuming 'measure' is an implicit action and is represented by prop_0, and 'prop_1' is as given, then both prop_0 and prop_1 must always be true.
The requirement states that the system must always satisfy both prop_0 and prop_1. In LTL, to state that both hold at all times, we use 'G (prop_0 & prop_1)'.

The requirement states that the system shall 'prop_0 and prop_1'. This means that both prop_0 and prop_1 must always hold true in all situations. In LTL, the 'globally' (G) operator is used to indicate that a property holds at all points in time. The conjunction of the two atomic propositions, prop_0 and prop_1, is expressed with '&', meaning both must always be satisfied.
The requirement specifies that both prop_0 and prop_1 should always hold. In LTL, this is encoded using the globally operator with the conjunction of the two atomic propositions.
The requirement states that both prop_0 and prop_1 should always hold throughout the execution (globally). This is expressed in LTL as 'G (prop_0 & prop_1)'.
